data.title: Asian FX, stocks rally as dollar retreats on Fed rate cut bets
data.description:
Key points:
  • Asian FX rally on Fed rate cut bets, dollar weak
  • Taiwan dollar, Korean won gain 0.4% each Friday
  • Regional stocks climb led by Manila, Taipei - up 1%

Several Asian currencies gained ground on Friday as the dollar retreated on mounting expectations the Federal Reserve will cut rates this month, while equities climbed after Wall Street's overnight surge to fresh record highs.

South Korea's won and Taiwan's dollar spearheaded the regional advance, each climbing 0.4% against the greenback. The Taiwan dollar rose for a third day after suffering seven consecutive sessions of declines.

In Thailand, parliament was set to chose a new prime minister after a court's dismissal of Paetongtarn Shinawatra as prime minister. The Thai baht strengthened 0.2% and Bangkok equities added 1%.

Overall, however, trade was thin with markets in Malaysia and Indonesia closed for public holidays.

Markets have all but priced in a quarter-point Fed rate cut at next week's policy meeting, with traders expecting 60 basis points of reductions by year-end.

The U.S. dollar index fell 0.2% to 98.11 as traders awaited jobs data later in the day expected to firm up the case for a cut. The Fed is due to meet on September 16 and 17.

"We anticipate the Federal Reserve will ease policy over the coming months, providing room for Asian central banks to cut rates to support growth amid trade headwinds," MUFG forex strategist Lloyd Chan said. "Against a backdrop of a weaker US dollar, we favour the yen, rupiah, and ringgit."

Expectations of an easier monetary environment have supported global equities, and the S&P 500 rose 0.8% on Thursday to finish at a record high.

That helped regional equities post broad gains, led by Manila shares , which rose 1% in their strongest performance in more than a week. Taipei stocks TWSE:TAIEX also climbed 1%, notching their third consecutive session of advances.

Taiwan's benchmark has gained 0.6% for the week, while Manila's index has eked out a 0.1% weekly advance after three straight weeks of declines.

The MSCI gauge of emerging market currencies (.MIEM00000CUS) was little changed this week, while a gauge of emerging Asian equities (.MIMS00000PUS) advanced over 1%, on track for its best week since August 11.

Though Indonesian markets had some respite this week, they could face headwinds again as deadly protests over lawmakers' bonuses escalated after police killed a motorcycle taxi driver during demonstrations.

Rights groups report 10 deaths and over 1,000 injuries in clashes with security forces.

The rupiah gained 0.4% over four sessions on broad dollar weakness after losing more than 1% due to the protests last week. Jakarta stocks rose 0.5% this week, after losing 0.4% last week as investors focused on expected Fed rate cuts.

HIGHLIGHTS:

** Indonesia to discuss fuel supply issue with private distributors

** China's central bank to inject 1 trillion yuan

data.title: UK house prices rise in August, Halifax says
data.description:
British house prices rose 0.3% in August, leaving them 2.2% higher than a year earlier, figures published by mortgage lender Halifax showed on Friday.

Economists polled by Reuters had forecast prices would rise 0.1% on the month and be 2% higher than the year before.

British house prices have been rising more slowly than consumer price inflation in recent months, after a surge in the first quarter of this year when buyers sought to take advantage of the final months of a tax break on property purchases.

August data from rival mortgage lender Nationwide on Monday showed that prices unexpectedly dropped 0.1%, causing annual house price inflation to slow to 2.1% from 2.4%.

Property website Rightmove - which advertises the vast majority of British homes for sale - said there had been a rise in sales in July, as sellers dropped their initial asking prices by more than usual due to a glut of property on offer.

Figures from the Bank of England showed mortgage approvals picked up to a six-month high in July.

However, industry body RICS said some buyers had appeared cautious that property taxes on more expensive homes would rise in finance minister Rachel Reeves' annual budget, which will take place on November 26.

Separate data from Rightmove, released earlier on Friday, showed that average rents for newly advertised tenancies were 3% higher than a year earlier at a record 1,577 pounds a month.

data.title: Saab receives order for Giraffe 4A radar systems from a country in Latin America
data.description:
Saab

Saab has received an order for Giraffe 4A radar systems with associated services from a country in Latin America. The order value is approximately SEK 550 million.

The mobile, self-contained, digital sensor solution Giraffe 4A increases the customer’s land-based air surveillance capabilities, and enables software-based updates.

“We are proud to announce the first contract of the land-based Giraffe 4A to Latin America. This milestone marks a significant step in expanding our presence in the region with advanced medium range radar. This capability will provide our customer with state-of-the-art air surveillance capability”, says Carl-Johan Bergholm, head of Saab’s business area Surveillance.

Giraffe 4A is a multi-functional radar system that offers users a range of advanced capabilities. With the ability to identify and track a high number of objects simultaneously, it is well-suited for both air and sea surveillance.

Contact

Saab Press Centre

+46 (0)734 180 018

presscentre@saabgroup.com

Saab is a leading defence and security company with an enduring mission, to help nations keep their people and society safe. Empowered by its 26,000 talented people, Saab constantly pushes the boundaries of technology to create a safer and more sustainable world. Saab designs, manufactures and maintains advanced systems in aeronautics, weapons, command and control, sensors and underwater systems. Saab is headquartered in Sweden. It has major operations all over the world and is part of the domestic defence capability of several nations.
